description: One of the most iconic prairie wildflowers, producing large daisy-like purple blooms with raised orange-brown cones. Extremely adaptable and long-lived.
careNotes: Drought tolerant once established. Deadhead to extend bloom or leave seed heads for winter bird food.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratified seed in spring. Transplant plugs spring through early summer.

description: A cheerful, prolific bloomer with bright golden-yellow petals surrounding a dark brown center cone. Short-lived perennial that self-sows freely.
careNotes: Very easy to grow. Tolerates poor soil and drought. May need division every few years.
plantingInstructions: Direct sow in fall or spring. Germinates readily without stratification. Space plugs 12 inches apart.

description: Brilliant orange flower clusters make this the showiest milkweed. Essential host plant for Monarch butterflies with a deep taproot.
careNotes: Do not transplant once established due to deep taproot. Needs well-drained soil. Slow to emerge in spring.
plantingInstructions: Sow cold-stratified seed in spring or direct sow in fall. Plant plugs in spring, water sparingly.

description: Fragrant lavender flower heads attract a wide range of pollinators. Aromatic foliage has a minty scent and was used historically for tea.
careNotes: Spreads by rhizomes; may need containment in small gardens. Tolerates drought and clay soil. Susceptible to powdery mildew in humid conditions.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify for spring planting. Space plugs 18 inches apart.

description: A towering native with massive domed clusters of dusky-pink flowers. Provides dramatic late-summer structure and is a butterfly magnet.
careNotes: Prefers consistent moisture. Can be cut back in early June to reduce height. Rarely needs staking in full sun.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs or potted plants in spring. Sow seed in fall for natural stratification. Space 24-36 inches apart.

description: Brilliant scarlet flower spikes are a hummingbird favorite. One of the few true red native wildflowers in the Chicago region.
careNotes: Requires consistent moisture. Short-lived but self-sows in favorable conditions. Mulch in winter to protect basal rosettes.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring near water features or in rain gardens. Surface-sow tiny seeds; they need light to germinate.

description: The primary Monarch butterfly host plant with fragrant pink-purple flower clusters. Produces ornamental seed pods with silky floss.
careNotes: Spreads aggressively by rhizomes; best for large areas or contained beds. Extremely tough once established.
plantingInstructions: Cold-stratify seed 30 days before spring sowing, or direct sow in fall. Space plugs 18-24 inches apart.

description: A well-behaved milkweed for garden settings with clusters of rose-pink flowers. Does not spread aggressively like Common Milkweed.
careNotes: Prefers moist to wet soil but adapts to average garden conditions. Slow to emerge in spring.
plantingInstructions: Cold-stratify seed or sow in fall. Plant plugs in spring in moist areas or rain gardens.

description: Produces masses of deep violet-purple daisy flowers in fall, providing essential late-season nectar. One of the showiest fall-blooming natives.
careNotes: Can be pinched back in June to reduce height and encourage bushier growth. Thrives in rich, moist soil.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify for spring. Plant plugs spring through early fall. Space 24 inches apart.

description: Elegant blue-purple flowers on smooth, waxy blue-green stems. More drought tolerant and less aggressive than New England Aster.
careNotes: Extremely drought tolerant. Does not require staking. Attractive blue-green foliage all season.
plantingInstructions: Direct sow in fall or cold-stratify seed. Space plugs 18 inches apart in well-drained soil.

description: A compact, mounding aster covered in blue-violet blooms in late fall. Aromatic foliage and excellent for borders and rock gardens.
careNotes: Very drought tolerant. Naturally mounding habit rarely needs support. Cut back in late spring for denser form.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring or fall. Sow seed in fall. Thrives in lean, dry soils.

description: Graceful drooping pale-pink petals distinguish this from Purple Coneflower. The true Chicago-region native Echinacea with deep taproot.
careNotes: Extremely drought tolerant. Do not overwater or over-fertilize. Leave seed heads for goldfinches.
plantingInstructions: Requires 60+ days cold stratification. Direct sow in fall for best results. Space 12-18 inches apart.

description: Unusual spiky white globe flowers and yucca-like leaves create bold architectural interest. A distinctive prairie indicator species.
careNotes: Very drought tolerant once established. Deep taproot makes it difficult to transplant. Excellent cut flower, fresh or dried.
plantingInstructions: Cold-stratify seed 60 days or sow in fall. Plant young plugs in spring; taproot resents disturbance later.

description: Dense spikes of bright magenta-purple flowers bloom from top to bottom. A signature tallgrass prairie species and Monarch nectar source.
careNotes: Prefers moist clay soils. May lean in part shade; stake or plant with supportive grasses.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify 60 days. Plant corms or plugs in spring. Space 12-18 inches apart.

description: Rounded button-like flower heads of rosy-purple spaced along tall stems. More drought tolerant than Prairie Blazing Star.
careNotes: Thrives in sandy or gravelly soils. Very low maintenance once established.
plantingInstructions: Direct sow in fall or cold-stratify seed. Plant corms or plugs in spring in well-drained soil.

description: Enormous sandpaper-textured basal leaves send up tall stalks with bright yellow sunflower-like blooms. A bold architectural prairie plant.
careNotes: Very long-lived once established. Deep taproot makes it extremely drought tolerant. Give it room.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall; needs cold stratification. Slow to establish but very persistent. Patience required.

description: Named for its deeply lobed basal leaves that orient north-south. A magnificent tallgrass prairie plant reaching 10 feet with yellow flowers.
careNotes: Very slow to establish from seed. Deep taproot makes it supremely drought tolerant. Avoid transplanting mature plants.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall for natural stratification. Be patient; may take 3-5 years to bloom from seed.

description: Flat-topped clusters of small white button flowers on sturdy stems. Long-blooming, reliable prairie workhorse.
careNotes: Very adaptable and low maintenance. Tolerates clay and drought. Good filler plant in prairie plantings.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify. Easy from plugs planted in spring. Space 18 inches apart.

description: One of the earliest blooming prairie forbs with bright yellow flat-topped flower clusters. Host plant for Black Swallowtail butterflies.
careNotes: Very adaptable to various conditions. Self-sows gently. One of the few natives blooming in early spring.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall; requires cold stratification. Plant plugs spring or fall. Tolerates light shade.

description: Clusters of fragrant pink to lavender flowers in late spring fill the gap between early and mid-season bloomers.
careNotes: Good air circulation reduces mildew. Well-drained soil is essential. Pair with early-blooming grasses.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring. Seed requires cold stratification. Space 12 inches apart in well-drained soil.

description: Flat-topped clusters of bright gold flowers provide critical late-season nectar. One of the most garden-worthy goldenrods; does not cause allergies.
careNotes: Does not spread aggressively like some goldenrods. Tolerates drought and poor soil. Pairs beautifully with asters.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify. Plant plugs in spring. Space 18 inches apart.

description: The most ornamental goldenrod with dense plume-like clusters of bright yellow flowers. Well-behaved in garden settings.
careNotes: Does not spread aggressively. Tolerates some drought. Good cut flower.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify. Plant plugs in spring in well-drained soil.

description: Three-petaled blue to violet flowers open in the morning and close by afternoon. Grass-like foliage adds texture all season.
careNotes: Cut back after flowering to refresh foliage. Very adaptable to different conditions. May self-sow.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring or fall. Sow seed in fall. Easy to establish.

description: Elegant swept-back petals resemble tiny rockets in flight. A spring ephemeral that goes dormant by midsummer.
careNotes: Goes dormant after flowering; mark location to avoid disturbing. Plant with later-emerging species to fill gaps.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall; requires cold stratification. Plant plugs in spring. Avoid soggy winter soils.

description: Nodding pink flowers mature into feathery seed heads resembling wisps of smoke. A charming low-growing groundcover for dry prairies.
careNotes: Needs excellent drainage. Semi-evergreen foliage turns reddish in fall. Slow to establish but long-lived.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify 60 days. Plant plugs in spring in well-drained soil.

description: The signature tallgrass prairie grass, growing up to 8 feet. Turkey-foot seed heads and blue-green stems turn coppery-bronze in autumn.
careNotes: Burns well in managed prairies. Cut back in late winter before spring growth. Tolerates a wide range of soils.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in spring or fall. Plant plugs spring through early summer. Space 24 inches apart.

description: Fine-textured fountain of emerald green foliage turns golden-orange in fall. Airy seed heads have a distinctive popcorn-like fragrance.
careNotes: Extremely long-lived and drought tolerant. Slow to establish but worth the wait. Rarely needs division.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or spring. Plant plugs in spring. Space 18-24 inches apart for a continuous groundcover effect.

description: Blue-green summer foliage turns brilliant mahogany-red in fall with fluffy white seed heads. The most widely distributed native grass in North America.
careNotes: Thrives in lean, dry soils. Too much fertility causes flopping. Cut back in late winter.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in spring or fall. Easy from plugs. Space 12-18 inches apart in well-drained soil.

description: A versatile warm-season grass with airy panicles and excellent fall color ranging from gold to burgundy. Provides winter structure.
careNotes: Very adaptable to wet or dry conditions. Cut back in late winter. May spread in moist soils.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in spring or fall. Plant plugs spring through early summer. Space 24-36 inches apart.

description: Elegant golden plumes top blue-green foliage that turns deep amber in autumn. A stately tallgrass prairie companion to Big Bluestem.
careNotes: Clump-forming and well-behaved. Cut back in late winter. Tolerates clay and some drought.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in spring or fall. Plant plugs in spring. Space 18-24 inches apart.

description: Distinctive oat-like seed heads dangle from one side of the stem. A compact, warm-season bunchgrass excellent for dry sites and borders.
careNotes: Very drought tolerant. Low-growing and non-aggressive. Cut back in late winter.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in spring. Easy from plugs. Space 10-12 inches apart.

description: A fine-textured cool-season bunchgrass with silvery-green flower spikes in early summer. One of the first grasses to green up in spring.
careNotes: Prefers well-drained soils. Goes semi-dormant in hot summers. Good for dry prairies.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or early spring. Plant plugs in spring. Space 8-10 inches apart.

description: A clumping prairie sedge with attractive winged seed heads. Provides fine texture in dry to mesic prairie plantings.
careNotes: Low maintenance once established. Cut back in late winter if desired.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or plant plugs in spring. Space 12 inches apart.

description: A fine-textured, low-growing sedge that forms an excellent lawn alternative in dry shade. Semi-evergreen, forming a dense turf.
careNotes: Mow once or twice a year or leave natural. Spreads slowly by rhizomes. No fertilizer needed.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs 8-12 inches apart in spring or fall. Water first season; once established, extremely low maintenance.

description: A tough, clumping wetland sedge ideal for rain gardens and detention basins. Dense spiky seed heads resemble fox tails.
careNotes: Tolerates periodic flooding and clay soil. Self-sows in favorable wet conditions.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall on moist soil. Plant plugs in spring in rain gardens or wet swales.

description: Distinctive palm-like leaf arrangement creates a tropical look in shade gardens. Spreads to form attractive colonies.
careNotes: Excellent shade groundcover for moist areas. Tolerates heavy clay. Cut back in late winter.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring or fall. Space 18 inches apart in moist shade areas.

description: Bright yellow sunflowers brighten woodland edges and partial shade areas. Spreads by rhizomes to form cheerful colonies.
careNotes: Can be aggressive in good conditions; use where spreading is welcome. Tolerates dry shade once established.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring or fall. Sow seed in fall. Space 24 inches apart.

description: Delicate pink-lavender flowers and deeply cut palmate leaves are a spring woodland highlight. Foliage turns reddish in fall.
careNotes: Very adaptable shade plant. Self-sows gently. Good companion for spring ephemeral bulbs.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring or fall in humus-rich soil. Sow seed in fall. Space 12-15 inches apart.

description: Nodding red-and-yellow flowers with distinctive spurs attract hummingbirds in spring. Delicate blue-green foliage adds grace to shade gardens.
careNotes: Short-lived perennial that self-sows freely. Tolerates rocky or sandy soil. May get leaf miner; cut back damaged foliage.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify. Plant plugs in spring. Thrives in well-drained, partly shaded sites.

description: Clusters of nodding light-blue bell flowers above neatly arranged ladder-like foliage. An early spring woodland gem.
careNotes: Prefers rich, moist woodland soil. Foliage remains attractive through summer in shade. Self-sows gently.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring or fall in humus-rich shade. Sow seed in fall for natural cold stratification.

description: Tall spikes of intense blue flowers rival any garden perennial. More robust and longer-lived than its red cousin, Cardinal Flower.
careNotes: Keep moist. Self-sows reliably. Longer-lived than Cardinal Flower in garden conditions.
plantingInstructions: Surface-sow tiny seeds in fall or spring. Plant plugs in moist shade or along water features.

description: One of the earliest spring wildflowers with pristine white blooms that emerge wrapped in blue-green leaves. A true spring ephemeral treasure.
careNotes: Goes dormant by midsummer. Plant with ferns and sedges to fill the gap. Do not disturb once planted.
plantingInstructions: Plant rhizomes in fall, 1-2 inches deep. Prefers rich, humusy woodland soil. Division is the best propagation method.

description: Clusters of nodding sky-blue trumpet flowers open from pink buds in spring. Goes completely dormant by early summer.
careNotes: Goes fully dormant by June; mark location. Plant with hostas, ferns, or sedges for continuous cover.
plantingInstructions: Plant bare roots in fall. Sow seed in fall. Prefers rich, moist floodplain-type soil.

description: Arching stems with paired dangling white bell flowers create elegant woodland sweeps. Dark blue berries attract birds in fall.
careNotes: Slowly spreads by rhizomes to form colonies. Very shade tolerant. Foliage turns golden in autumn.
plantingInstructions: Plant bare roots or plugs in fall or spring. Set rhizomes 2 inches deep in rich, woodsy soil.

description: The quintessential spring woodland wildflower with three large white petals aging to pink. Extremely slow to establish but worth the wait.
careNotes: Very slow to mature from seed (5-7 years to bloom). Protect from deer browse. Never dig from the wild.
plantingInstructions: Plant nursery-propagated bulbs in fall, 3-4 inches deep. Sow fresh seed in fall; germination takes two winters.

description: Heart-shaped velvety leaves form a lush groundcover in deep shade. Hidden jug-shaped maroon flowers are pollinated by ground-dwelling insects.
careNotes: Excellent shade groundcover. Spreads slowly by rhizomes. Prefers rich, slightly acidic woodland soil.
plantingInstructions: Plant bare roots or plugs in spring or fall. Space 12 inches apart for groundcover. Keep moist first season.

description: Spiky seed heads resemble bottle brushes on arching stems. One of the best native grasses for shade and woodland edges.
careNotes: Short-lived but self-sows reliably. Tolerates dry shade. Good for naturalizing woodland areas.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or spring. Plant plugs in spring. Easily establishes in shaded sites.

description: Elegant violet-blue iris flowers grace wetland edges and rain gardens in late spring. Sword-like foliage provides vertical accent all season.
careNotes: Needs consistently moist to wet soil. Tolerates standing water. Divide every 3-4 years to maintain vigor.
plantingInstructions: Plant rhizomes or plugs in spring along pond edges, rain gardens, or wet swales. Space 12-18 inches apart.

description: Brilliant buttercup-yellow flowers signal spring in wet meadows and stream banks. Glossy kidney-shaped leaves form attractive mounds.
careNotes: Requires consistently wet soil. May go partially dormant in summer heat. Excellent for pond margins.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs or divisions in spring in wet soil or shallow water. Space 12 inches apart.

description: Aromatic iris-like foliage with a sweet cinnamon scent when crushed. Inconspicuous finger-like flower spikes. Excellent for shoreline stabilization.
careNotes: Thrives in shallow water or saturated soil. Spreads by rhizomes. Fragrant foliage is its main ornamental feature.
plantingInstructions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring along pond edges or in rain gardens. Tolerates up to 6 inches of standing water.

description: Pencil-thin spikes of violet-blue flowers bloom from bottom to top over many weeks. A pollinator powerhouse for wet-mesic sites.
careNotes: Self-sows freely in moist areas. Very low maintenance. Tolerates heavy clay.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify for spring. Plant plugs in moist soil. Space 18 inches apart.

description: Distinctive perfoliate leaves clasp the stem, topped with flat white flower clusters buzzing with pollinators. A wetland workhorse.
careNotes: Tolerates wet clay soil. Attracts a huge diversity of native bees and beneficial insects.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall on moist soil surface. Plant plugs in spring in rain gardens or wet meadows.

description: Vivid deep purple flower clusters on strong iron-tough stems. One of the most intensely colored native wildflowers.
careNotes: Tolerates wet clay soils. Very tough once established. Can be pinched in June to reduce height.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify. Plant plugs in spring in moist to wet areas. Space 18-24 inches apart.

description: A tall, robust grass for wet prairies and shoreline stabilization. Sharp-edged leaves and comb-like seed heads turn golden in fall.
careNotes: Spreads vigorously by rhizomes; use only where aggressive spreading is acceptable. Excellent for erosion control.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring in wet areas. Contain with barriers if needed. Space 24-36 inches apart.

description: Round, upright dark green stems form dense tufts along water edges. Small brown flower clusters emerge from the side of stems.
careNotes: Thrives in standing water or saturated soil. Cut back dead stems in early spring. Provides year-round structure.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring in standing water up to 4 inches deep, or in saturated rain garden soil.

description: A highly adaptable shrub with clusters of white flowers, reddish seed capsules, and exfoliating bark that peels in papery layers.
careNotes: Extremely tough and adaptable. Prune after flowering if needed. Responds well to renewal pruning.
plantingInstructions: Plant bare root or potted shrubs in spring or fall. Tolerates almost any soil condition.

description: A large multi-stemmed shrub producing edible hazelnuts in frilly husks. Early spring catkins are among the first pollen sources for bees.
careNotes: Forms thickets by suckering. Good for screening and wildlife hedges. Nuts attract squirrels and jays.
plantingInstructions: Plant bare root or potted in spring or fall. Needs cross-pollination for nut production. Space 6-8 feet apart.

description: A compact shrub with dense clusters of frothy white flowers. Nitrogen-fixing roots improve soil. Important host plant for spring azure butterflies.
careNotes: Drought tolerant once established. Deep root system; difficult to transplant once mature. Do not overwater.
plantingInstructions: Plant young potted plants in spring. Sow seed after scarification and cold stratification. Space 3-4 feet apart.

description: Brilliant red stems provide vivid winter color. White flower clusters yield white berries beloved by birds. Fast-growing and adaptable.
careNotes: Prune oldest stems to the ground in late winter to maintain red stem color. Spreads by suckering.
plantingInstructions: Plant bare root or potted in spring or fall. Tolerates wet clay. Space 4-6 feet apart.

description: Deciduous holly with spectacular bright red berries persisting through winter on bare branches. Requires a male pollinator for fruit.
careNotes: Need one male per 6-8 females for berries. Prefers acidic to neutral, moist soil. Minimal pruning needed.
plantingInstructions: Plant potted specimens in spring or fall. Ensure a male pollinator is within 50 feet. Mulch well.

description: Perhaps the most adaptable native shrub. White berry clusters on red stems attract dozens of bird species. Gray winter bark is ornamental.
careNotes: Extremely adaptable to nearly any condition. Spreads by suckering to form thickets; good for hedgerows.
plantingInstructions: Plant bare root or potted in spring or fall. Extremely easy to establish. Space 4-6 feet apart.

description: Clusters of white spring flowers, glossy black berries, and brilliant red fall foliage. One of the most ornamental and versatile native shrubs.
careNotes: Extremely adaptable. Tolerates wet clay and drought. Suckering habit is manageable. Fall color is outstanding.
plantingInstructions: Plant potted or bare root in spring or fall. Very easy to establish. Space 3-5 feet apart.

description: Large flat-topped clusters of creamy white flowers followed by dark purple berries edible when cooked. A fast-growing multi-stemmed shrub.
careNotes: Very fast growing. Prune out old canes annually. Berries must be cooked before eating. Tolerates wet soil.
plantingInstructions: Plant bare root or potted in spring. Prefers moist, fertile soil. Space 6-8 feet apart.

description: A compact prairie sub-shrub with silvery-gray foliage and spikes of tiny deep purple flowers with orange anthers. Nitrogen-fixing legume.
careNotes: Extremely drought tolerant. Deep root system; benefits from managed burns. Slow to establish but very persistent.
plantingInstructions: Scarify seed before sowing. Plant young plugs in spring. Very slow to establish; be patient.

description: The iconic savanna oak with massive spreading canopy and deeply furrowed bark. Produces large fringed acorns. Extremely long-lived and tough.
careNotes: Very drought tolerant once established. No pruning needed. Provides habitat for hundreds of insect species that feed birds.
plantingInstructions: Plant acorns in fall or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring. Deep taproot; plant young trees for best results.

description: An adaptable oak for wet sites with attractive bicolored leaves and peeling bark. Tolerates periodic flooding better than most oaks.
careNotes: Tolerates poorly drained clay and occasional flooding. Good urban tree. Supports hundreds of caterpillar species.
plantingInstructions: Plant balled-and-burlapped or potted trees in spring. Tolerates clay and wet soil. Water first two seasons.

description: A fast-growing oak with outstanding red fall color. One of the most commonly planted native shade trees in the Chicago region.
careNotes: Faster growing than white oaks. Tolerates urban conditions well. Avoid pruning in spring when oak wilt is active.
plantingInstructions: Plant balled-and-burlapped trees in spring or fall. Water deeply the first two years.

description: One of the earliest trees to bloom, with clouds of white flowers before leaves emerge. Edible blueberry-like fruits and fiery orange-red fall color.
careNotes: Relatively pest-free. Multi-stemmed form can be pruned to a single trunk. Fruits are delicious fresh or in pies.
plantingInstructions: Plant balled-and-burlapped or potted in spring or fall. Adaptable to most soil types.

description: Stunning pink-purple flowers coat bare branches in early spring before heart-shaped leaves emerge. A beloved small understory tree.
careNotes: Prune in summer to avoid canker diseases. At northern edge of range in Chicago; site in protected locations.
plantingInstructions: Plant balled-and-burlapped or potted in spring. Protect from harsh winter winds. Mulch well.

description: A large shade tree with fragrant pale yellow flowers that are a major honey source. Heart-shaped leaves and a stately form.
careNotes: May sucker from the base; remove suckers as needed. Provides deep shade. Attracts many pollinators when in bloom.
plantingInstructions: Plant balled-and-burlapped trees in spring. Water deeply the first two years. Prefers rich, moist soil.

description: Among the most spectacular native trees for fall color, with glossy leaves turning brilliant scarlet. Dark blue fruits attract many bird species.
careNotes: Slow growing. Taproot makes transplanting difficult; plant young trees. Prefers acidic soil but adapts.
plantingInstructions: Plant young container-grown trees in spring. Difficult to transplant once established due to taproot.

description: A vigorous woody vine with five-leaflet leaves turning brilliant crimson in fall. Dark blue berries are critical fall food for migrating birds.
careNotes: Very vigorous; needs strong support or space to climb. Annual pruning keeps it manageable. Tolerates any condition.
plantingInstructions: Plant potted vines in spring or fall near structures, fences, or trees. Easy to establish.

description: Clusters of tubular coral-red flowers attract hummingbirds all summer. A well-behaved native vine, unlike invasive Japanese honeysuckle.
careNotes: Not aggressive like invasive honeysuckles. Semi-evergreen. Provide trellis or fence support.
plantingInstructions: Plant potted vines in spring or fall at the base of a trellis, fence, or arbor. Mulch well.

description: A twining vine prized for ornamental orange-and-red fruit clusters used in fall arrangements. Needs male and female plants for berries.
careNotes: Ensure you have the native species, not invasive Oriental bittersweet. Needs strong support. Prune to control size.
plantingInstructions: Plant potted vines of both sexes in spring. Provide sturdy trellis, fence, or tree to climb.

description: The most elegant native fern with delicate fan-shaped fronds on glossy black stems. Creates a beautiful fine-textured groundcover in shade.
careNotes: Prefers rich, consistently moist woodland soil. Protect from wind. Slowly forms expanding colonies.
plantingInstructions: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring in rich, humusy shade. Keep moist until established.

description: Graceful, finely cut fronds in an upright vase shape. One of the most adaptable and vigorous native ferns for shade gardens.
careNotes: Easy to grow in moist shade. Deciduous; cut back old fronds in early spring before new fiddleheads emerge.
plantingInstructions: Plant potted or bare root ferns in spring in rich, moist shade. Space 18-24 inches apart.

description: The largest native fern with distinctive cinnamon-brown fertile frond tips resembling flower clusters. Turns golden in autumn.
careNotes: Needs consistently moist to wet acidic soil. Spectacular in bog gardens or along stream banks.
plantingInstructions: Plant potted or bare root ferns in spring in boggy or consistently wet soil. Mulch with leaf mold.

description: Bright yellow daisy flowers on wiry stems with deeply lobed, finger-like leaves. Spreads to form attractive colonies in dry prairies.
careNotes: Spreads by rhizomes; good for naturalizing. Very drought tolerant.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify. Plant plugs in spring in well-drained soil. Space 18 inches apart.

description: Pairs of upper leaves fuse to form water-holding cups at the stem. Tall yellow sunflower-like blooms attract goldfinches to the seeds.
careNotes: Large plant; give it space. Cups collect rainwater used by birds and insects. Self-sows.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall. Plant plugs in spring in moist soil. Space 24-36 inches apart.

description: Spires of pea-like blue flowers above palmate leaves. The sole host plant for the endangered Karner Blue butterfly. Nitrogen-fixing legume.
careNotes: Requires sandy, well-drained soil. Does not tolerate heavy clay or wet conditions. Inoculate seed with rhizobium.
plantingInstructions: Scarify and inoculate seed before sowing in fall. Plant plugs in spring in sandy soil only.

description: Cylindrical flower heads with tiny rose-purple florets bloom from bottom to top. A nitrogen-fixing legume that enriches prairie soils.
careNotes: Very drought tolerant. Deep taproot; do not transplant once mature. Good in mixes with grasses.
plantingInstructions: Scarify seed and sow in spring or fall. Plant plugs in spring. Inoculate with rhizobium for best results.

description: Tall spires of white pea-like flowers above blue-green foliage. Forms a large, shrub-like clump that is ornamental all season. Black seed pods rattle in wind.
careNotes: Extremely long-lived. Deep taproot; do not transplant once established. Slow to mature but persistent.
plantingInstructions: Scarify seed before sowing. Plant young plugs in spring; they are slow to establish. Space 36 inches apart.

description: Drooping racemes of soft cream flowers on a low, spreading mound. An early-blooming prairie legume that fixes nitrogen in the soil.
careNotes: Deep taproot; plant young and do not disturb. Very drought tolerant. Slow to establish.
plantingInstructions: Scarify and cold-stratify seed. Plant young plugs in spring. Be patient during establishment.

description: Spikes of tubular pink flowers that can be pushed to any angle and stay put (hence the name). Vigorous late-summer bloomer.
careNotes: Spreads aggressively by rhizomes in rich soil. Contain or use in naturalized areas. Cut back in June for shorter plants.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring. Easy from seed sown in fall. Give it room to spread or use barriers.

description: Tall spikes of white tubular flowers with faint purple striping. Semi-evergreen basal foliage often has a maroon tint. Easy and adaptable.
careNotes: Very low maintenance. Tolerates clay and drought. Self-sows moderately. Good cut flower.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify. Plant plugs in spring. Space 12-18 inches apart.

description: Elegant candelabra-like spires of white flowers rise above whorled foliage. A refined prairie plant with strong vertical architecture.
careNotes: Prefers moist soil in full sun. Does not spread aggressively. Excellent architectural plant for borders.
plantingInstructions: Surface-sow tiny seed in fall. Plant plugs in spring in moist soil. Space 18-24 inches apart.

description: Low-growing groundcover with white flowers and tiny, intensely flavored red strawberries. Spreads by runners to form a dense mat.
careNotes: Excellent groundcover. Spreads by runners. Fruits are small but very flavorful. Attractive to wildlife.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring or fall. Space 12 inches apart. Spreads quickly by runners.

description: Nodding clusters of pink to white bell-shaped flowers on wiry stems. Grass-like foliage has a mild onion scent. A charming prairie bulb.
careNotes: Very drought tolerant. Self-sows gently. Deer and rabbit resistant due to onion flavor.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or plant bulbs 2 inches deep in spring. Space 6-8 inches apart.

description: Airy sprays of tiny greenish flowers above a rosette of scalloped leaves. The native coral bells alternative for sun gardens.
careNotes: More sun tolerant than woodland heucheras. Long-lived. Good border or edging plant.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring or fall. Surface-sow tiny seeds in fall. Space 12 inches apart.

description: Brilliant scarlet flowers on sticky stems are a hummingbird magnet. A rare and striking prairie wildflower once more common in Illinois.
careNotes: Needs well-drained soil. Short-lived but self-sows in favorable conditions. An Illinois endangered species.
plantingInstructions: Surface-sow seed in fall. Plant plugs in spring in well-drained soil. Avoid wet winter soils.

description: Dense spikes of magenta-purple flowers bloom top to bottom. The species behind most garden cultivars. Excellent for rain gardens and wet meadows.
careNotes: Prefers moist soil. More compact than Prairie Blazing Star. Excellent cut flower.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify. Plant corms or plugs in spring in moist soil.

description: Tiny yellow flowers dot bare branches in early spring. Aromatic leaves and red berries in fall. Host plant for Spicebush Swallowtail butterfly.
careNotes: Best in moist, rich woodland soil. Aromatic when bruised. Dioecious; need male and female for berries.
plantingInstructions: Plant potted or bare root in spring or fall in moist shade. Space 6-8 feet apart.

description: White snapdragon-like flowers shaped like turtle heads bloom in late summer. Host plant for Baltimore Checkerspot butterfly.
careNotes: Needs consistently moist soil. Does not tolerate drought. Good rain garden plant in part shade.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring in moist shade. Surface-sow seed in fall. Space 12-18 inches apart.

description: A cool-season grass with feathery purplish seed heads. Excellent for wet meadows, rain gardens, and shoreline stabilization.
careNotes: Spreads by rhizomes; best for larger naturalized areas. Tolerates wet clay. Cut back in late fall.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring in wet meadow or rain garden settings. Space 18-24 inches apart.

description: Whorls of lavender-spotted flowers with soft downy leaves. A fragrant woodland mint that attracts many native bees.
careNotes: Drought tolerant in shade. Self-sows modestly. Aromatic foliage deters deer and rabbits.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or plant plugs in spring in dry to mesic woodland settings.

description: The last shrub to bloom, with fragrant spidery yellow flowers on bare branches in late fall. Golden fall foliage. A unique woodland understory plant.
careNotes: Slow growing. Tolerates deep shade. Multi-stemmed form; rarely needs pruning.
plantingInstructions: Plant potted or balled-and-burlapped in spring or fall in woodland settings. Water first season.

description: Drooping, flattened oat-like seed heads on bamboo-like stems. One of the best ornamental grasses for shade. Turns copper-bronze in fall.
careNotes: Self-sows prolifically; remove seed heads before dropping if spread is unwanted. Excellent for shade.
plantingInstructions: Plant plugs in spring or fall in part shade. Easy from seed. Space 18-24 inches apart.

description: Golden-yellow flowers with a subtle sweet fragrance. Blooms later and taller than common Black-eyed Susan. A true perennial rather than biennial.
careNotes: More reliably perennial than R. hirta. Tolerates clay. Excellent late-season bloomer.
plantingInstructions: Sow seed in fall or cold-stratify. Plant plugs in spring. Space 18-24 inches apart.
